metadata.title: Type III Error Detector — Wrong Problem, Right Solution
metadata.topic: CONDUCTING AI — TYPE III ERROR (WRONG PROBLEM)
metadata.note: CLI 10-beat spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'NikBearBrownOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

Type I error is a false positive. Type II is a false negative. Type III — coined by statistician Howard Raiffa — is solving the wrong problem correctly. AI is particularly good at Type III errors: it optimizes the objective you stated, not necessarily the one you meant. Ask it to reduce support ticket volume and it might suggest hiding the contact button. Technically correct. Completely wrong. The reframing test forces you to state the problem three different ways and check whether the solutions diverge.
beats.visual_intent: title card: Type III error as the AI risk — optimizing the stated objective, not the intended one

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Type I error is a false positive. Type II is a false negative. Type III — coined by statistician Howard Raiffa — is solving the wrong problem correctly. AI is p

beats.narration_text: Ask for the detector. In the terminal: claude — write a Python script that accepts a problem statement, calls the Anthropic API to generate three reframings using zoom-out, zoom-in, and change-actor moves, then for each pair runs a distinctness test — a test solution that passes one reframing but fails another. Print a table with reframing, move, test solution, and pass/fail flags. Use claude-3-5-haiku.

props.command:
claude "Write a Python script: accepts a problem statement;
calls Anthropic API to generate 3 reframings using
zoom-out / zoom-in / change-actor moves; for each pair runs
distinctness test; prints table: reframing, move,
test solution, pass/fail flags. Use claude-3-5-haiku."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. The reframings come back as a JSON array. The distinctness test passes one reframing's solution against each other reframing. If all solutions pass all reframings, they collapsed to the same problem — Type III risk. The divergence is the signal.

props.code:
# type3_detector.py — Claude Code output
import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def detect_type3(problem_statement):
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model='claude-3-5-haiku-20241022',
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{'role':'user','content':
            f'Generate 3 reframings of: "{problem_statement}"\n'
            f'Moves: zoom-out, zoom-in, change-actor.\n'
            f'For each, provide a test_solution that passes '
            f'that reframing but might fail another.\n'
            f'Return JSON: {{"reframings": [...]}}'
        }]
    )
    # reframings = json.loads(client.messages.create(...).content[0].text)["reframings"]
    return json.loads(resp.content[0].text)['reframings']

beats.narration_text: Run it on the support-ticket problem: reduce support ticket volume. Three reframings. Zoom-out: why do users need support at all? Test solution — hide the button — passes this reframing but fails zoom-in. Zoom-in: which ticket type is most expensive? Test solution — automate top three queries — passes zoom-in but fails change-actor. Change-actor: what constraint blocks self-service? Test solution — improve search UX — passes all three. Row three is not distinct. The solutions converged. That convergence is the Type III warning.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_ReframingTable: 3-row pass/fail matrix, row 3 flagged not-distinct in CRIMSON

beats.narration_text: Row three collapsed. Ask for a revision: generate a fourth reframing using a constraints move — what physical or organizational constraint is blocking the solution — and re-run the distinctness test.

props.command:
claude "update type3_detector.py:
  -> add constraints move as 4th reframing
  -> re-run distinctness test for all 4 reframings
  -> flag any that still collapse"

beats.narration_text: Re-run. Four reframings now. The constraints move produces a distinct test solution — one that passes its reframing but fails the others. Three of four are distinct. The tool surfaces the problem structure.
beats.visual_intent: updated 4-reframing pass/fail table, 3 of 4 distinct, 1 still flagged

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Re-run. Four reframings now. The constraints move produces a distinct test solution — one that passes its reframing but fails the others. Three of four are dist

beats.narration_text: Type III error is not a logic failure. It is a framing failure. The AI solved the problem you stated — and the problem you stated was wrong. The reframing test does not guarantee you found the right problem; it guarantees the reframings are actually different from each other, which is necessary before any of them is worth solving.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: reframing distinctness as Type III prevention; 'necessary not sufficient'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Type III error is not a logic failure. It is a framing failure. The AI solved the problem you stated — and the problem you stated was wrong. The reframing test

beats.narration_text: Your move: take your current highest-priority problem statement and run it through this script. Look at the distinctness column. If all reframings converge, you are probably solving the wrong problem. And the right problem is in the gap between the reframings.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) run your top problem through reframing test, 2) look for non-distinct rows, 3) act on the gap
